annotations.label: Wave

anchor.snippet: a wave is a propagating dynamic disturbance

annotations.note: The physical notion of a propagating disturbance is not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Decomposition into sinusoidal plane waves

anchor.snippet: complicated waves can generally be decomposed as the sum of many sinusoidal plane waves

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Fourier-series decomposition into exponentials/sinusoids (L² convergence), the rigorous analog, but not the physical plane-wave decomposition.

annotations.label: Wave (microscopic)

anchor.snippet: waves are changes in the value of physical property at a point in space

annotations.note: A physical/microscopic description with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Wave as field disturbance

anchor.snippet: a wave is disturbance in a field resulting from delayed response to adjacent disturbances

annotations.note: Physical field-disturbance description, not formalized.

annotations.label: Wave as a function

anchor.snippet: that maps a point in space and time onto a field

annotations.note: A function from space-time to a field value is just a generic map; no dedicated wave definition exists.

annotations.label: Wave domain

anchor.snippet: the wave's domain is then a subset

annotations.note: The wave-specific domain notion has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Superposition of waves

anchor.snippet: The properties at that point are the sum of the properties of each component wave at that point.

annotations.note: The physical superposition principle is not stated as such, beyond generic additivity.

annotations.label: Wave spectrum

anchor.snippet: A wave spectrum is a representation that describes how the energy of a sea surface

annotations.note: An oceanographic energy-spectrum concept with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Family of waves

anchor.snippet: one may describe such a family of waves by a function

annotations.note: A parametrized family of physical waves is not formalized.

annotations.label: Standing wave in a recorder

anchor.snippet: the sound pressure inside a recorder that is playing a

annotations.note: A physical acoustics example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Drum skin vibration

anchor.snippet: the vibrations of a drum skin after a single strike depend only on the distance

annotations.note: A physical vibration example, not formalized.

annotations.label: Temperature in a metal bar

anchor.snippet: the temperature in a metal bar when it is initially heated at various temperatures

annotations.note: A heat-conduction example with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Heat equation

anchor.snippet: it is called the heat equation in mathematics

annotations.note: The heat equation appears only as an unfilled entry in docs/undergrad.yaml, with no declaration in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: Wave equation

anchor.snippet: wave equation in mathematics, even though it describes only one very special kind of waves

annotations.note: The wave equation appears only as an unfilled entry in docs/undergrad.yaml, with no declaration in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: Traveling transverse wave on a string

anchor.snippet: Consider a traveling transverse wave (which may be a pulse ) on a string (the medium).

annotations.note: A physical wave-on-string example, not formalized.

annotations.label: d'Alembert's formula

anchor.snippet: more generally, by d'Alembert's formula

annotations.note: d'Alembert's solution of the wave equation is not present in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: Duhamel's principle

anchor.snippet: General solutions are based upon Duhamel's principle

annotations.note: Duhamel's principle for inhomogeneous PDEs is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Waveform travels at velocity v

anchor.snippet: the wave shaped like the function F will move in the positive x -direction at velocity v

annotations.note: The traveling-waveform statement F(x-vt) is a physical claim with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Spatial and temporal periodicity

anchor.snippet: a snapshot of the wave at a given time t finds the wave varying periodically in space

annotations.note: Function.Periodic captures periodicity in a single variable, but the joint spatial/temporal wave periodicity is not formalized.

annotations.label: Envelope of a wave

anchor.snippet: The outline of the variation in amplitude is called the envelope of the wave.

annotations.note: The amplitude envelope of a wave is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: The outline of the variation in amplitude is called the envelope of the wave.

annotations.label: Envelope moves with group velocity

anchor.snippet: showing that the envelope moves with the group velocity and retains its shape

annotations.note: This group-velocity propagation statement has no Mathlib counterpart.

anchors.snippet: showing that the envelope moves with the group velocity and retains its shape

annotations.label: Phase velocity

anchor.snippet: Phase velocity is the rate at which the phase of the wave propagates in space

annotations.note: Phase velocity is a physical quantity not defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Phase velocity is the rate at which the phase of the wave propagates in space

annotations.label: Group velocity

anchor.snippet: Group velocity is a property of waves that have a defined envelope

annotations.note: Group velocity is a physical quantity not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sine wave

anchor.snippet: is a periodic wave whose waveform (shape) is the trigonometric sine function

annotations.note: The sine function and its 2π-periodicity are formalized (Real.sin_periodic), but the physical 'sine wave' is not.

annotations.label: Linear combination of sine waves

anchor.snippet: the result is another sine wave of the same frequency

annotations.note: Angle-addition identities (Real.sin_add/cos_add) underlie the harmonic-addition result, but the statement that a·sin+b·cos is a single sinusoid is not a named lemma.

annotations.label: Plane wave

anchor.snippet: A plane wave is a kind of wave whose value varies only in one spatial direction.

annotations.note: The plane-wave physical concept is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Standing wave

anchor.snippet: is a wave whose envelope remains in a constant position

annotations.note: The standing-wave physical concept is not formalized.

annotations.label: Sum of counter-propagating waves

anchor.snippet: The sum of two counter-propagating waves (of equal amplitude and frequency) creates a standing wave

annotations.note: The sum-to-product identity sin_add_sin yields exactly the standing-wave factorization 2·sin(kx)·cos(ωt), though waves themselves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Soliton

anchor.snippet: A soliton or solitary wave is a self-reinforcing wave packet that maintains its shape

annotations.note: Solitons are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Longitudinal vs transverse propagation

anchor.snippet: we can distinguish between longitudinal wave and transverse waves

annotations.note: The longitudinal/transverse distinction is a physical classification with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Helmholtz decomposition solution

anchor.section: Reflection of plane waves in a half-space
anchor.snippet: The frequency domain solution can be obtained by first finding the Helmholtz decomposition of the displacement field

annotations.note: The Helmholtz decomposition appears only in docs/1000.yaml, with no declaration in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: SV wave reflection

anchor.snippet: the plane SV wave reflects back to the domain as a P and SV waves

annotations.note: Elastodynamic SV-wave reflection is not formalized.

annotations.label: P wave reflection

anchor.snippet: the P incidence, in general, reflects as the P and SV wave

annotations.note: Elastodynamic P-wave reflection is not formalized.

annotations.label: Phase velocity formula

anchor.snippet: The phase velocity is given as

annotations.note: The phase-velocity formula v=ω/k is a physical relation not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The phase velocity is given as

annotations.label: Dispersion relationship

anchor.snippet: both are related through the dispersion relationship

annotations.note: Dispersion relations are physical and not formalized.

anchors.snippet: both are related through the dispersion relationship

annotations.label: Non-dispersive waves

anchor.snippet: the waves are called non-dispersive, since all frequencies travel at the same phase speed

annotations.note: The non-dispersive condition is a physical concept not formalized.

annotations.label: Group velocity from dispersion gradient

anchor.snippet: is called the group velocity and is determined from the gradient of the dispersion relation

annotations.note: The group-velocity-as-gradient relation is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: is called the group velocity and is determined from the gradient of the dispersion relation

annotations.label: Classification of transmission media

anchor.snippet: Such media can be classified into one or more of the following categories

annotations.note: A physical classification of media with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Absorption

anchor.snippet: A material which absorbs a wave's energy, either in transmission or reflection

annotations.note: Wave absorption is a physical concept not formalized.

annotations.label: Law of reflection

anchor.snippet: the angle made by the incident wave and line normal to the surface equals the angle made by the reflected wave

annotations.note: The law of reflection is a physical optics statement not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Refraction

anchor.snippet: Refraction is the phenomenon of a wave changing its speed.

annotations.note: Refraction is a physical phenomenon with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Snell's law

anchor.snippet: are related to the refractive indices of the two materials by Snell's law

annotations.note: Snell's law is not present in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: Diffraction

anchor.snippet: A wave exhibits diffraction when it encounters an obstacle that bends the wave

annotations.note: Diffraction is a physical phenomenon not formalized.

annotations.label: Superposition principle

anchor.snippet: the field quantities describing those waves add according to the superposition principle

annotations.note: The physical superposition principle is not formalized beyond generic linearity/additivity.

annotations.label: Polarization

anchor.snippet: The phenomenon of polarization arises when wave motion can occur simultaneously in two orthogonal directions.

annotations.note: Wave polarization is a physical concept not formalized.

annotations.label: Linear polarization

anchor.snippet: A transverse wave is linearly polarized if it oscillates in only one direction or plane.

annotations.note: Linear polarization is a physical concept not formalized.

annotations.label: Dispersion

anchor.snippet: A wave undergoes dispersion when either the phase velocity or the group velocity depends on the wave frequency.

annotations.note: Dispersion is a physical phenomenon not formalized.

annotations.label: Doppler effect

anchor.snippet: is the change in frequency of a wave in relation to an observer who is moving relative to the wave source

annotations.note: The Doppler effect is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mechanical wave

anchor.snippet: A mechanical wave is an oscillation of matter

annotations.note: Mechanical waves are physical and not formalized.

annotations.label: Speed of wave on a string

anchor.snippet: The speed of a transverse wave traveling along a vibrating string ( v ) is directly proportional to the square root of the tension of the string

annotations.note: The string wave-speed formula v=√(T/μ) is a physical relation not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Acoustic wave

anchor.snippet: Acoustic or sound waves are compression waves that propagate through gases, liquids, solids and plasmas.

annotations.note: Acoustic waves are physical and not formalized.

annotations.label: Gravity wave

anchor.snippet: Gravity waves are waves generated in a fluid medium or at the interface between two media

annotations.note: Fluid gravity waves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Body wave

anchor.snippet: Body waves travel through the interior of the medium along paths controlled by the material properties

annotations.note: Seismic body waves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Seismic wave

anchor.snippet: Seismic waves are waves of energy that travel through the Earth's layers

annotations.note: Seismic waves are physical and not formalized.

annotations.label: Shock wave

anchor.snippet: When a wave moves faster than the local speed of sound in a fluid , it is a shock wave.

annotations.note: Shock waves are not formalized.

annotations.label: Shear wave

anchor.snippet: Shear waves are body waves due to shear rigidity and inertia.

annotations.note: Shear waves are physical and not formalized.

annotations.label: Gravitational wave

anchor.snippet: Gravitational waves are disturbances in the curvature of spacetime

annotations.note: Gravitational waves are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metachronal wave

anchor.snippet: Metachronal wave refers to the appearance of a traveling wave produced by coordinated sequential actions.

annotations.note: Metachronal waves are a biological phenomenon with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Electromagnetic wave

anchor.snippet: An electromagnetic wave consists of two waves that are oscillations of the electric and magnetic fields.

annotations.note: Electromagnetic waves are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maxwell: fields satisfy wave equation

anchor.snippet: the electric and magnetic fields satisfy the wave equation both with speed equal to that of the speed of light

annotations.note: Neither Maxwell's equations nor the resulting wave equation are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schrödinger equation

anchor.snippet: The Schrödinger equation describes the wave-like behavior of particles in quantum mechanics

annotations.note: The Schrödinger equation has no declaration in Mathlib/.

annotations.label: Dirac equation

anchor.snippet: The Dirac equation is a relativistic wave equation detailing electromagnetic interactions.

annotations.note: The Dirac equation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: de Broglie hypothesis

anchor.snippet: Louis de Broglie postulated that all particles with momentum have a wavelength

annotations.note: The de Broglie hypothesis is a physical postulate not formalized.

annotations.label: de Broglie wavelength

anchor.snippet: this wavelength is called the de Broglie wavelength

annotations.note: The de Broglie wavelength λ=h/p is a physical quantity not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Electron de Broglie wavelength

anchor.snippet: the electrons in a CRT display have a de Broglie wavelength of about

annotations.note: A numerical physics example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Wave packet

anchor.snippet: de Broglie proposed a superposition of different wavelengths ranging around a central value in a wave packet

annotations.note: The wave-packet concept is a physical object not formalized.

annotations.label: Gaussian wave packet

anchor.snippet: the wave packet is often taken to have a Gaussian shape and is called a Gaussian wave packet

annotations.note: The Gaussian function itself is formalized (ProbabilityTheory.gaussianPDFReal, and complex Gaussians in the Fourier file), but the physics wave-packet object is not.

annotations.label: Fourier transform of a Gaussian

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform of a Gaussian is itself a Gaussian.

annotations.note: fourierIntegral_gaussian proves the Fourier transform of a Gaussian exp(-b·x²) is again a Gaussian.

annotations.label: Fourier/Heisenberg uncertainty for wave packets

anchor.snippet: a narrow range of wavelengths is necessary to produce a localized wave packet, and the more localized the envelope, the larger the spread in required wavelengths

annotations.note: The Fourier-analytic / Heisenberg uncertainty principle relating spatial spread and wave-vector spread is not formalized in Mathlib.
